verses.text: At the end of every seven years thou shalt make a release. Every creditor shall release that which he hath lent. He shall not exact it of his brother; for it is called the LORD's release. Of a foreigner thou mayest exact it again. Guidance: A national reset button every seven years. Debt erased — not just delayed, but cancelled. Generational poverty got a ceiling built into the law. The LORD's release: the debt cancellation belongs to Him, not to the lender's generosity. He just delegates the unclenching.

Save when there shall be no poor among you; for the LORD shall greatly bless thee in the land which the LORD thy God giveth thee. Only if thou carefully hearken unto His voice. The LORD will bless thee; thou shalt lend unto many nations, not borrow; reign over many nations, not be reigned over. Guidance: A 'save when' tucked into the text — IF Israel obeys, there would be no poor. The covenant economy is generous enough to end need. The condition is hearkening. Disobedience produces the very inequity the law was designed to dissolve.

If there be among you a poor brother, thou shalt not harden thine heart, nor shut thine hand. Open thine hand wide, lend him sufficient for his need. Beware that there be not a wicked thought, saying, The year of release is at hand; thou givest him nought. Give him, thine heart not grieved; the LORD shall bless thee in all thy works. The poor shall never cease out of the land: open thine hand wide unto thy needy. Guidance: God anticipates the calculator. Six months before the release, the loan looks like a loss — so the lender hesitates. The law says: don't. The poor are always with you, not as a problem to manage but as a permanent doorway to obedience.

If thy brother be sold unto thee, and serve thee six years; in the seventh year thou shalt let him go free. Thou shalt not let him go empty: furnish him liberally out of thy flock, thy floor, thy winepress; of that wherewith the LORD hath blessed thee, give him. Remember that thou wast a bondman in Egypt, and the LORD redeemed thee. Guidance: A six-year limit on bondage, and a sending-off package on the way out. Don't return him to poverty — load his arms with flock, grain, wine. Egypt is the reason: remember what it was like to leave with nothing, and how God did not send YOU empty.

If he shall say, I will not go away — because he loveth thee and thine house — then take an aul, thrust it through his ear unto the door, and he shall be thy servant for ever. It shall not seem hard when thou sendest him away free; for he hath been worth a double hired servant to thee. Guidance: Some servants chose to stay. The pierced ear was a visible mark: I belong to this house. It was love, not chains, that kept him. And the master is told: don't begrudge the release — he gave you double the value. Mercy on the way out is not loss.

All firstling males of herd and flock thou shalt sanctify unto the LORD: not work the firstling of bullock, nor shear firstling of sheep. Eat it before the LORD year by year, in the place He shall choose. If there be any blemish — lame, blind, ill blemish — sacrifice it not unto the LORD; eat it within thy gates. Only thou shalt not eat the blood; pour it on the ground as water. Guidance: The firstborn animal belongs to God, not the plow. It cannot work, cannot be sheared — its first profit goes back. If blemished, eat it at home; God will not be honored with second-quality. The blood line holds. Worship is the best, given first.
